original_description: Test-Driven Development. Iron Law: no production code without a failing test first. Use when: writing tests, TDD, test first, implementing features, fixing bugs, hotfix. Trigger on: "escribe tests", "TDD", "test first", "implementa feature", "arregla el bug", "bug fix".
best_description: Test-Driven Development. Iron Law: no production code without a failing test first. Use when: writing tests, TDD, test first, implementing features, fixing bugs, hotfix. Trigger on: "escribe tests", "TDD", "test first", "implementa feature", "arregla el bug", "bug fix".
final_description: Activate when someone is about to write code and tests must come before production code — the Red-Green-Refactor cycle. Explicit triggers: user says "TDD" or "test first"; wants tests written against a spec/interface before the implementation exists. Implicit triggers: any bug fix or hotfix (a failing test capturing the regression always comes first, even for emergencies); implementing a feature or method "correctly" or "the right way." This is a development workflow skill, not a test generator — it guides the implementation process. Skip for: testing theory questions, test framework setup, code review of existing code, and documentation tasks.

history.description: Test-Driven Development. Iron Law: no production code without a failing test first. Use when: writing tests, TDD, test first, implementing features, fixing bugs, hotfix. Trigger on: "escribe tests", "TDD", "test first", "implementa feature", "arregla el bug", "bug fix".

history.description: Invoke this skill whenever a user wants to implement any feature, method, or module using Test-Driven Development — the Red-Green-Refactor cycle where failing tests come before production code. Use for: any request that explicitly mentions TDD or test-first; writing tests for an interface, class, or spec before the implementation exists; fixing bugs or hotfixes where a failing test should capture the regression before the fix is applied; implementing something "correctly from the start" or "the right way." Skip for general testing theory questions, test framework setup/configuration, code review, documentation, or architecture planning that doesn't involve writing code yet.
